pi_items.source: Task JSON, confirmed via principlesofeconometrics.com POE5 dataset repository and embedded data in install_gretl.sh
Code analysis (setup_task.sh, verifier.py) and data analysis showing income < 15 vs income >= 15 split
Direct Python analysis of food.gdt data: counted 23 observations with income < 15 in the 40-observation dataset
Direct Python analysis of food.gdt data: counted 17 observations with income >= 15 in the 40-observation dataset
OLS regression on low-income group (food_exp vs income) computed via scipy.stats.linregress: slope = 14.5912, which falls within [8.0, 22.0]
OLS regression on high-income group (food_exp vs income) computed via scipy.stats.linregress: slope = 9.2557, which falls within [4.0, 18.0]
Confirmed in task.json metadata and referenced in setup_task.sh and export_result.sh scripts

privileged_info_summary: The food.gdt dataset contains 40 real household survey observations from Hill, Griffiths, and Lim's Principles of Econometrics 5th Edition. When split by income threshold (15 $100-units): low-income group (income < 15) has N=23 with OLS slope coefficient 14.59 (range 8.0-22.0 verified), and high-income group (income >= 15) has N=17 with OLS slope coefficient 9.26 (range 4.0-18.0 verified). Total dataset spans 40 observations with income from $3.69 to $26.75 hundred-dollar units.
